skill.name: shader-dev
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through GLSL shader design, WebGL2 adaptation, and real-time visual effect implementation.
skill.summary: Create and adapt GLSL shader effects with focused technique guides and WebGL2 rules.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but targeted review found GLSL documentation, Markdown links, and WebGL examples rather than active command execution or exfiltration. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found; residual risk is low because the community skill contains copyable HTML and JavaScript snippets.

low_findings.title: False positive: shader math read as shell execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown and GLSL tutorial content, such as inline backticked function names and equations. No Ruby backtick execution or shell command invocation was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are shader concepts, equations, and prose. They do not contain executable shell syntax or a runtime that would execute commands.

low_findings.title: False positive: rendering terms read as network and filesystem access
low_findings.description: The network finding is the phrase texture fetch, and the path traversal findings are relative Markdown links to reference documents. These are documentation patterns, not code that opens files or performs network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show prose and relative documentation links. No fetch API call, socket use, file read, or path construction was present.

low_findings.title: Static HTML examples include inline script snippets
low_findings.description: Some technique files include standalone WebGL examples with script tags and static innerHTML fallback messages. The reviewed snippets do not use user-controlled input, but copied examples should still be handled as code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script patterns are real documentation snippets, but the innerHTML assignments reviewed are static fallback messages. This is a minor publishing concern, not confirmed exploit behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical heuristic combination dismissed after context review
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner combined command, network, credential, and filesystem indicators across a large shader corpus. Targeted review found shader terms, Markdown reference links, and browser demo code, with no evidence of credential access or data exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious combination is explainable by rendering vocabulary and documentation links. Confidence is not higher because the corpus is large and only representative high-signal locations were reviewed.

content.value_statement: Shader development often fails because examples mix ShaderToy syntax, WebGL2 rules, and performance limits. This skill routes requests to focused GLSL techniques and adaptation guidance for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Routes shader requests to specific technique guides for geometry, lighting, simulation, post-processing, texture sampling, and audio.
Explains ShaderToy-to-WebGL2 conversion rules for fragment output, uniforms, version headers, and main wrappers.
Provides GLSL implementation patterns for ray marching, signed distance fields, noise, particles, fluids, water, terrain, and shadows.
Includes performance budgets for loop counts, sampling limits, and browser rendering constraints.
Documents common WebGL2 and GLSL compilation pitfalls with concrete correction guidance.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a shader compiler or visual preview tool.
Generated shaders still need browser or GPU testing for correctness and performance.
Examples are focused on GLSL and WebGL2, not native graphics APIs such as Vulkan or Metal.
The skill may require manual adaptation for engine-specific material systems.

use_cases.title: Prototype a ShaderToy Effect
use_cases.description: Create a fragment shader concept using ray marching, procedural noise, palettes, and post-processing guidance.

use_cases.title: Port GLSL to WebGL2
use_cases.description: Convert ShaderToy-style code into a standalone WebGL2 page with correct uniforms, shader headers, and canvas setup.

use_cases.title: Debug Visual Artifacts
use_cases.description: Use documented WebGL2 pitfalls, loop budgets, texture sampling rules, and normal estimation techniques to isolate rendering issues.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Shader
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a complete effect
prompt_templates.prompt: Use shader-dev to create a WebGL2 fragment shader for a glowing procedural orb. Keep the loops small and explain the main uniforms.

prompt_templates.title: Convert ShaderToy Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Adapting an existing shader to WebGL2
prompt_templates.prompt: Use shader-dev to convert this ShaderToy-style fragment shader into WebGL2. Apply the version, output, uniform, and main wrapper rules.

prompt_templates.title: Combine Techniques
prompt_templates.prompt: Use shader-dev to design an ocean sunset scene with Gerstner waves, atmospheric scattering, soft shadows, and tone mapping.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Complex Shader
prompt_templates.prompt: Use shader-dev to review this ray marching shader for WebGL2 errors, loop budget risks, normal estimation problems, and sampling artifacts.

output_examples.input: Create a ray-marched crystal cave shader.
output_examples.output: A structured plan that selects ray marching, SDF modeling, domain repetition, lighting, shadows, and post-processing. The response explains scene construction, uniforms, performance limits, and WebGL2 adaptation steps.

output_examples.input: Why is my WebGL2 shader showing a white screen?
output_examples.output: A debugging checklist covering shader version placement, missing main wrappers, uniform null locations, JavaScript declaration order, type mismatches, and browser console checks.

output_examples.input: Make my texture mapping less repetitive.
output_examples.output: Technique guidance for biplanar mapping, per-tile random offsets, derivative-aware sampling, mipmap stability, and tradeoffs between quality and texture fetch count.

best_practices: Start with the routing table, then read only the technique files that match the requested visual effect.
Keep generated shaders within the documented loop and sampling budgets before adding visual complexity.
Test generated WebGL2 output in a browser console and fix compilation errors before tuning aesthetics.
anti_patterns: Do not paste ShaderToy code into WebGL2 without adding the required version, output variable, uniforms, and main wrapper.
Do not combine many expensive sampling techniques before measuring frame time and reducing loop counts.
Do not treat documentation snippets as production security guidance for untrusted HTML or JavaScript input.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete shaders?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide complete GLSL and WebGL2 shader creation, but the output still needs browser testing.

faq.question: Can it convert ShaderToy examples to WebGL2?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes rules for shader headers, outputs, uniforms, main wrappers, and common WebGL2 syntax changes.

faq.question: Does it include ray marching and SDF guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers ray marching, 2D and 3D signed distance fields, CSG operations, repetition, and SDF optimization.

faq.question: Can it help with performance problems?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents loop limits, sampling budgets, and common causes of slow or frozen browser rendering.

faq.question: Is this a rendering engine?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation and generation skill for AI coding tools, not a runtime renderer.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
